name: Cornucopia
description: The Cornucopia, also known as the horn of plenty, is a symbol of abundance and nourishment, commonly depicted as a large horn overflowing with fruits, vegetables, flowers, nuts, and other provisions. Its origins are rooted in Greek mythology, with several stories explaining its creation. One prominent myth involves the infant Zeus, who was hidden in a cave on Mount Ida to protect him from his father, Cronus. He was nursed by the nymph Amalthea, who possessed a magical goat whose horn would provide endless nourishment. Another tale recounts how Heracles (Hercules) broke off one of the horns of the river god Achelous during a wrestling match. The Naiads then filled the horn with fruits and flowers, transforming it into a symbol of prosperity. The Cornucopia represents prosperity, wealth, and the endless bounty of the earth. It symbolizes gratitude for the gifts of nature and the rewards of hard work. In modern times, it is often associated with harvest festivals like Thanksgiving and is used as a decorative element to represent abundance and good fortune. The Cornucopia's enduring appeal lies in its simple yet powerful message of hope and plenty, reminding us of the potential for prosperity and the importance of appreciating the blessings in our lives. It serves as a potent reminder of nature's generosity and the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, wherein abundance follows periods of scarcity. Its image continues to be used in art, literature, and popular culture, solidifying its place as an iconic symbol of prosperity across various cultures and time periods.
subtitle: Horn of Plenty: Symbol of Abundance
material: Goat Horn (or other material depending on the myth)
origin: Created from the horn of Amalthea's goat or broken from the river god Achelous.
currentLocation: Symbolic, exists in art and representation.

keyMyths.title: Zeus and Amalthea
keyMyths.description: The Cornucopia originated from the horn of a magical goat that nursed the infant Zeus, providing him with endless nourishment in a cave on Mount Ida. This horn symbolized the abundance and prosperity that would follow Zeus throughout his reign.

keyMyths.title: Heracles and Achelous
keyMyths.description: Heracles broke off one of the horns of the river god Achelous during a wrestling match, which the Naiads then filled with fruits and flowers, transforming it into a symbol of plenty. This tale represents the triumph over nature's challenges resulting in prosperity.

sources.author: Hesiod
sources.description: Describes the origins of the gods and early Greek cosmology.
sources.text: Describes the origins of the gods and early Greek cosmology.

sources.author: Ovid
sources.description: A collection of mythological tales, including the story of Heracles and Achelous.
